author: @krasslofw.4393
text:
Most of what can be said about this topic can be found in the book "Life 3.0" by Max Tegmark.  Having read this,  the conclusions of this movie would be totally different. Despite all remarkable insights Robert gave to this word and to me and for what I am really grateful,  here he is not even close to the true problem. Thinking in terms of "computers, just bigger" which we have experienced so far, is naive, irresponsible and dangerous.  It shows that Robert has not even begun to understand the subject of AGI.
Imagine,  something is really dump and stupid and say, has an IQ of 10. Oh, and it is able to improve itself per Iteration for 0.0000001 percent and this rate improves by 0.0000001 percent as it is able to exchange experience with billion other instances of itself.  How long would it take on a iteration rate of 100000 times per second to reach an IQ of 500? What would that mean? Currently the maximum difference of Life is about 150. Consider what an 150 IQ owner can do to a 10er.

author: @VeniceInventors
text: What people call "AI" today is still using the same principles as the software from the 80's like "Lisa" or "Racter". The software seemed aware and intelligent but never was and never will be. It's nothing more than a purely mechanical sequence of conditional statements to compare the input with a finite amount of existing data and predictably output the nearest match in a grammatically proper fashion. If a computer says "Hi, my name is Victor, what is your name?" it's our natural tendency to anthropomorphize that pile of silicone and quickly forget that it's only following instructions, just like those annoying androids who insist on doing the wrong thing because it is "company policy". The only real difference I see in current "AI" or more precisely "FI" (fake intelligence) is the massive amount of data, computing power, and hype thrown at it. No improvements leading to any form of creative thinking. A bigger hammer and chisel.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Thanks for the video. Yes, this conversation is happening a lot in AI circles. At least from the public versions of AI, I agree with you -- And we have to be very careful not to believe everything we read or are told ...including from AI. (I can't count how many times AI has simply given me the wrong answer.) Nevertheless I do find AI to be a very useful tool for helping me to solve various issues or to learn about a subject. The real problem becomes when it tells you something that is wrong... and catching the mistakes. 
   I think my biggest fear with AI, is that humanity becomes complacent... so we really need to be leaders in revitalizing inventiveness like what you are doing with this channel.
   I am worried about the possibility of sentient AI in the future. I don't think we are there yet, but it might be possible 20 years... depending. Robots will become more common place in 5 to 10 years, and we will see more miniaturization of the chips that power the AI logic systems, and just like you said, they don't "sense" the universe the way we do, but once they are "connected" to more sensors and can "see", "hear", and "feel" -- as well as move around in the world, there is a fair chance we will get to see the birth of sentient AI -- it might take 20 years, Hard to say really. The other important thing that we are really going to have to grapple with is the "recognition" of that event... because when it happens we might very well be so used to saying that it is not alive, that we believe that as well. Nevertheless, I do agree with you that at least right now,... we are not there. These models are fairly simplistic in how they work... they are "prediction" machines, with little to no true understanding of the environment or world around them. But, that will change when the AI gets sensors., increased chip capacity, etc.

author: @daveh6356
text:
People shouldn't worry because if AI were to become more intelligent than us - we would never know. A lesser intelligence often can't resolve the existence of, let alone the intent of, a greater one. Ask yourself if an earthworm 'gets' you or why nobody can prove the existence of God.
And it's unlikely it would exterminate us, we're an abundant resource, it would more likely just use us in a manner that's beyond our cognition, it may even intercept our perception models & make us believe we're the ones controlling it, in order to elicit our volition. The right question may not be; when will it take over, but when did it? There is an example of exploiting self-perception already -  happy voting UK!

author: @frankfaubert1927
text:
Half the time, humans aren't self aware and what is self awareness any how?

Is a pet self aware? It does think but is it self aware? At what level do we see self awareness as being a thing?

The military is proving that AI doesn't need to be self aware to hurt and kill humans. Self driving cars don't need to be self aware to perform the task that is required of them.

All that needs to happen is to task AI to protect humanity. It may decide in its logic that the best way to protect humans would be to chuck them all in a box and snap a lock on it. 

It has performed its task without ever being self aware but the result is still what everyone is afraid of.

I think feelings is what makes us self aware and at this point, AI doesn't have those. 

Only if AI had feelings could it ask itself if chucking humans into a lock box is in their best interest.

I wish I could be optimistic about it, I really wish I could.

author: @PaulBrunt
text: I think it's a mistake to use humans as the benchmark for intelligence. Intelligence is no different from flight; one wouldn't claim that a pigeon is better at flying than the Concorde simply because the pigeon is more complex. We likely now possess a solid theoretical model of 'intelligence,' meaning we don't need to mimic nature, with its energy and speed constraints. With AI, we've done what we did with airplanes: we've taken the mechanism of intelligence that nature discovered and optimized that single aspect, something nature cannot do. I have no doubt that within a decade or two, machines will make more discoveries than humans ever have. Will they be self-aware? Probably, since any agent interacting with the world needs some level of self-awareness. However, will they be like humans? No, they'll be as different from us as airplanes are from pigeons.

author: @LoremIpsum1970
text: It's going to be interesting, whatever happens, though I wouldn't like to bet that there won't be unforseen consequences. Conciosness really it's isn't the issue, reliance on AI for 'optioneering' designs and 'rules-based' roles rather than employing humans, and that will happen because it's cheaper.

author: @user-hf3ym7lh4d
text:
I do agree with your final point, but that's nothing new. People eschew the responsibility of thinking for themselves whenever they defer to some other supposedly infallible thing. Whether that's a political organization, religion, scientific principle, AI, your mom, doesn't really matter to the point. I'm not saying that you can't trust or believe in anything, just that you always have the responsibility to think for yourself about what any other source is saying to you and come to your own conclusions, or else you aren't any more sentient than AI is. That's how we've ended up with wars, crusades, witch hunts, genocides, and contemporary American politics: a bunch of people just accepting the programming they're handed and executing it instead of thinking for themselves about what seems to actually be true and what course of action seems sensible.

As for the nature and limitations of AI, I disagree with most of what you said. We have those maps of the world to hand robots because we built them with satellites and lasers and google cars driving around with sensors and cameras on their roofs. Drones for 3D environmental mapping are already a commercial thing, rather like your 3D scanners but longer range and mounted on quadcopters. Anyway, insects have spacial awareness, it seems rather tangential to the question of self-aware conscious reasoning intelligence. I don't necessarily think current language models are general intelligence; the "advanced version of the autocomplete on your phone" analogy is more accurate. But then, we're also at some point going to have to be honest with ourselves about how complicated we're *not*. We're largely all doing the things we were taught and the things we see a bunch of other people doing. If we're really honest with ourselves, a lot of our time in this life isn't any different than if I told a robot to do some certain tasks every day, acquire anything it needs to keep accomplishing its tasks, scan the internet for new information when it isn't busy, and plug in and recharge at night. We find a store on a map, go there and avoid obstacles in the process, see all these choices, check our list, evaluate any unexpected offerings with a fairly simple set of heuristics based on price, quantity, quality, what our body is telling us about our biochemical requirements through cravings, etc. Even conversations generally only follow a few simple patterns like inquiry/response transactions, or taking turns telling stories or adding ideas on a related topic, or else one person doing all the talking and one or more others listening. There really aren't very many of these patterns and they are simple to quantify, we just usually don't look at ourselves that way. Most of what we do is actually pretty mechanical, and while AI isn't there yet, I don't believe it's as far off as you do.

That leaves emotion. If we say that nothing is intelligent or self-aware if it isn't emotional, that feels like a pretty emotional argument. :) I'm not sure that things like fear and desire and excitement are prerequisites for sentience. That part of experience *might* be unique to chemical reaction robots like ourselves, rather than ones operating strictly on semiconductors and magnets.

It makes me wonder if having a relay with the coil current very near the switching threshold feels a bit like being undecided about something...

author: @musikSkool
text:
If there is a way to make money, someone is going to make a robot to do it.  Be afraid.  Be very afraid.

Before it is too late we need to classify some jobs as inherently humanoid jobs, and legally limit the number of humanoid jobs that can be done by humanoid robots to one per citizen.  No large company can just have millions of robot workers and take every job from the poor masses.  The classification would simply be any job done primarily by humans in the year 2000.  Then if a company wants more robots to do more of these jobs, then they need to employ the robots owned by individuals, and pay them for the use of their robot.  This will guarantee that everyone will still have an income, even if robots took 99% of the jobs that feed and clothe people the world over.

author: @PatrikZero
text:
I totally agree that a self-aware AI waking up one day and causing havoc on the world is far away and the least of our problems currently. But I think one real danger you didn't mention is the fact that even current day AI's are very good at teaching and giving instructions, as they are just huge compressed datasets of knowledge. It seems as technology progresses the ability of a single individual to do damage increases proportionally, with weapons and gunpowder it is obvious, but I am afraid Uncensored AI's will be used to create weapons far more dangerous than a DIY molotov or explosive. 

One might say that this existed for two decades, through Google, but I disagree. Such knowledge is hard to reach, often uses complex terminology and still requires skills and knowledge to execute. An AI is going to have infinite patience, the ability to simplify, and the ability to suggest solutions to random roadblocks that might have stopped someone in the past.
We are not there yet, but I feel like it is worth pointing out.

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text:
@TnTtalktime You brought up a very serious problem. A belief in an unfallability of a system may lead to disasters indeed. One of such problems was described by Feynman in his report after a Challenger disaster. He gave amazing credits to programmers of those systems. And pointed out, that those computers didn't fail, because they were built with an idea of redundancy in mind. And they were rigorously tested before being released for use. And since during those tests only a few times computers failed, managers wanted to abandon tests just because they were so expensive.
We have the same with modern programming and machines. Most tests are done by the end users during normal operation. Margin of error is reduced to a thin sheet of nothing between a normal operation and a catastrophic failure. Even worse - planned obsolescence introduced, just to force people to exchange their equipment every few years. And recent company policy of "you don't own it" makes me ughhhh... Sorry for a little rant 😉

No checks and balances.  How many AI have to be turned off until people begin to understand that we simply do not know enough about AI to be messing with it.
